coredata.dc:title: Gold nanoparticles modified reduced graphene oxide nanosheets based dual-quencher for highly sensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen
coredata.dc:creator: Kalkal, Ashish
Pradhan, Rangadhar
Packirisamy, Gopinath
coredata.dc:description: In the current scenario, the dominance of cancer is becoming a disastrous threat to mankind. Therefore, an advanced analytical approach is desired as the need of the hour for early diagnosis to curb the menace of cancer. In this context, the present work reports the development of nano surface energy transfer (NSET) based fluorescent immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) detection utilizing protein functionalized graphene quantum dots (anti-CEA/amine-GQDs) and a nanocomposite of nanostructured gold and reduced graphene oxide (AuNPs@rGO) as energy donor-acceptor pair, respectively. The obtained AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite has been characterized by different advanced analytical techniques. The functionality of the biosensor depends on quenching the fluorescence of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs donor species by AuNPs@rGO acceptor species, followed by the gradual recovery of GQDs' fluorescence after CEA addition. The efficient energy transfer kinetics have been envisaged by utilizing the AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite as a dual-quencher nanoprobe that revealed improved energy transfer and quenching efficiency (∼62 %, 88 %) compared to AuNPs (∼43 %, 81 %) as a single quencher. Further, the developed biosensing platform successfully detected CEA biomarker with notable biosensing parameters, including a wider linear detection range (0.001–500 ng mL−1), fast response time (24 min), and a significantly low detection limit (0.35 pg mL−1).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nano surface energy transfer (NSET)
Dual-quencher
Cancer diagnosis
Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA)
Nanocomposite

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Articles from the Special Issue on Nano-bio-engineered macromolecules and surfaces fabrication for biosensing applications; Edited by Pranjal Chandra

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (amine-GQDs)
One-pot in-situ synthesis of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite
Fabrication and mechanism of fluorescent immunosensing platform for CEA detection
Structural, optical, and morphological characterizations of GO and AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite
Optimization of experimental conditions for CEA detection
Analytical performance of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO-based immunosensing platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Government of India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: AK thanks the Ministry of Education, Government of India (GOI), for the fellowship. GP is grateful to the Department of Science and Technology (DST), GOI, Technology Innovation Hub (TIH) for Devices Materials and Technology Foundation, IIT Roorkee [A Section-8 Company: Divyasampark IHub for Devices Materials and Technology Foundation]. Sincere thanks to Centre for Nanotechnology and Institute Instrumentation Centre, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, for the various analytical facilities provided.

AK thanks the Ministry of Education, Government of India (GOI), for the fellowship. GP is grateful to the Department of Science and Technology (DST), GOI, Technology Innovation Hub (TIH) for Devices Materials and Technology Foundation, IIT Roorkee [A Section-8 Company: Divyasampark IHub for Devices Materials and Technology Foundation]. Sincere thanks to Centre for Nanotechnology and Institute Instrumentation Centre, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, for the various analytical facilities provided.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of procedure and mechanism of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO based fluorescent immunosensor for CEA detection.
(a) XRD pattern (b) UV–Vis spectra of GO (cyan line) and AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite (green line).
TEM images of (a) bare rGO nanosheets (b-d) AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite at different scales indicating the presence of hexagonal, triangular, and spherical-shaped AuNPs deposited on the rGO nanosheets.

(a) Fluorescence quenching spectra of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs with increasing concentrations of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite ranging from 0 to 100 μg mL−1 (b) Corresponding QE (c) Optimization of pH (d) Optimization of response time for the anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO immunosensing probe.

(a) Fluorescence response of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO based sensing platform for CEA detection (b) Pattern of recovered PL intensity with increasing log CEA concentration (Inset shows the calibration plot in the range of 0.001–500 ng mL−1, revealing the linear association of recovered fluorescence intensity with log CEA concentration) (c) Control experiment of amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO immunoprobe for CEA concentration 0.001–500 ng mL−1 (d) Interferent study of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO immunoprobe against various analytes known to be present in serum sample.

(a) Fluorescence response of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO based immunosensing platform for five different CEA spiked serum samples (b) Calibration curve between log CEA concentration and recovered PL intensity.
Comparison of different biosensing platforms for CEA detection.

head.ce:title: Gold nanoparticles modified reduced graphene oxide nanosheets based dual-quencher for highly sensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Uttarakhand 247667, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Uttarakhand-247667, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: iHub Divyasmapark, Technology Innovation hub, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Uttarakhand 247667, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: iHub Divyasmapark, Technology Innovation hub, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Uttarakhand-247667, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Uttarakhand 247667, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Uttarakhand-247667, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Uttarakhand 247667, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current scenario, the dominance of cancer is becoming a disastrous threat to mankind. Therefore, an advanced analytical approach is desired as the need of the hour for early diagnosis to curb the menace of cancer. In this context, the present work reports the development of nano surface energy transfer (NSET) based fluorescent immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) detection utilizing protein functionalized graphene quantum dots (anti-CEA/amine-GQDs) and a nanocomposite of nanostructured gold and reduced graphene oxide (AuNPs@rGO) as energy donor-acceptor pair, respectively. The obtained AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite has been characterized by different advanced analytical techniques. The functionality of the biosensor depends on quenching the fluorescence of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs donor species by AuNPs@rGO acceptor species, followed by the gradual recovery of GQDs' fluorescence after CEA addition. The efficient energy transfer kinetics have been envisaged by utilizing the AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite as a dual-quencher nanoprobe that revealed improved energy transfer and quenching efficiency (∼62 %, 88 %) compared to AuNPs (∼43 %, 81 %) as a single quencher. Further, the developed biosensing platform successfully detected CEA biomarker with notable biosensing parameters, including a wider linear detection range (0.001–500 ng mL−1), fast response time (24 min), and a significantly low detection limit (0.35 pg mL−1).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has become a major health problem and a significant barrier to increasing life expectancy worldwide [1]. It is currently the second most death-causing disease, accounting for around 10 million deaths in 2020 [2]. Early diagnosis can provide an efficient therapeutic response resulting in lesser morbidity and a higher survival rate [3]. In this regard, quantifying cancer-specific biomarkers with higher sensitivity and accuracy has been reported as an efficient remedy for early diagnosis, monitoring disease progression, designing the response to chemotherapeutic treatment, risk assessment, and accurate pre-treatment staging [4]. Biomarkers are chemical substances that are secreted by either cancer cells themselves or the specific response of the body to the occurrence of cancer in various biological fluids such as tears, urine, saliva, cerebral spinal fluid, and serum [5]. Extensive studies have identified various inflammatory, oxidative stress, proteinous, nucleic acid, circulating, and cytogenic biomarkers that could help in the early diagnosis of different cancer types [4]. An ideal cancer biomarker should have the ability to be assayed quantitatively, fast, longer, higher secretion in the biological fluid of origin, and increased clinical specificity and sensitivity [5].

Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) stands out most widely used biomarker. It is associated with the detection and monitoring of several cancers, including gastric carcinoma [6], breast [7], colorectal [8], and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) [9]. It is a kind of glycoprotein with a molecular mass of 180–200 KDa and is related to substances known as tumor-associated antigens. It is typically involved in cell adhesion and usually develops during fetal growth; however, its production ceases before birth [10]. There is a substantial difference in the serum CEA concentration of healthy beings and cancer patients [11]. In healthy humans, the serum CEA concentration reaches 0–2.5 ng mL−1 and at a higher level of 2–5 ng mL−1 in smokers [10]. However, elevated serum CEA concentration (≥20 ng mL−1) in patients greatly indicates the presence of cancer [9,12]. Currently, a number of different techniques, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), immunohistochemistry (IHC), chemiluminescence, radioimmunoassay (RIA), polymerase chain reaction (PCR), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and western blotting have been reported for biomarker detection. However, these conventional methods possess some inevitable limitations, such as tedious, time-consuming, and complicated procedures, the requirement of trained personnel, expensive and sophisticated instruments [13]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for the development of an ultrasensitive, user-friendly, cost-effective, and efficient technique for the rapid detection of cancer-specific biomarkers.

In the view of foregoing, the design and development of nanostructured materials-based biosensors have grown immense research interest for point-of-care (POC) diagnosis, which can detect the specific biomarkers present in various biological fluids [14–17]. With the advent of nanoscience and nanotechnology, these biosensors can provide multiple desirable characteristics, including higher sensitivity, good selectivity, low cost, fast response, miniaturization, portability, onsite and accurate detection [18–21]. In line with this, the recent progression in nano-surface energy transfer (NSET) based fluorescent biosensing platforms is also exerting immense interest for bioimaging, clinical diagnostics, biochemical analysis, and observing bio-molecular interactions owing to their rapid and reliable diagnostic ability, cost-effectiveness, high selectivity, inherent sensitivity and environmental friendliness [22–24]. The NSET mechanism theorized by Persson and Lang involves the non-radiative energy transfer from a photo-excited donor to a nearby nanomaterial surface when there is a substantial overlap between the emission and absorption spectra of the donor and acceptor, respectively [25]. In contrast to the widely known Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, the NSET mechanism reputes acceptor species as a nanometric surface (collection of many dipoles) instead of a point dipole [26]. Besides, in the NSET mechanism, the energy transfer phenomenon is operational over a distance up to 50 nm (more than twofold of the FRET), expanding the applicability of bio-molecular interaction detection in long-range systems [27]. Further, it has been reported that conventional FRET-based biosensing platforms entail high background signals and low signal-to-noise ratio [28]. In the quest for improved analytical performance and energy transfer efficiency, continuous efforts are being made toward exploring novel and efficient donor-acceptor pairs.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gradually evolved as a superior alternative to traditional fluorophores and energy donor species owing to their remarkable optoelectronic properties. Since their origin, these materials have engrossed tremendous attention due to the merits of tunable emission spectra, strong fluorescence, high quantum yield, good water solubility, long-term photostability, wide excitation spectra, and excellent biocompatibility [29–31]. Furthermore, the presence of surface functionalities in GQDs can facilitate the covalent immobilization of biomolecules [32,33]. Likewise, two-dimensional (2D) graphene derivatives and metal nanoparticles have attained considerable attention as proficient energy acceptor species owing to their ability to accept transferred energy even at a longer distance following the NSET phenomenon [34–36]. Due to larger surface area, improved multiplexing capability, flexible surface-modification capability, excellent water-solubility, etc., these nanomaterials-based energy acceptor species reveal superior quenching properties, thereby advancing detection sensitivity [28,37]. Extensive experimental studies and theoretical calculations have proved that energy transfer from a fluorescent donor to these nanomaterials follows the NSET mechanism based on 1/d4 distance dependence, where d is the distance between the donor-acceptor pair [34].

However, most of the reported NSET-based fluorescent biosensors use single energy acceptor or quencher, limiting the energy transfer capability and quenching efficiency [22,27]. Recently, it has been mentioned that compared to a single quencher, using a dual quencher in a single system can facilitate improved sensing characteristics [38–41]. In this context, Hirotsu et al. reported an improved Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) detection method using a dual-quencher instead of a single quencher in a reverse-transcription PCR assay. Introducing dual-quencher probes improved the detection sensitivity and reduced the background signal for SARS-CoV-2 detection [41]. Similarly, Huang et al. reported a dual quencher-based self-assembled fluorescence biosensor for the detection of inexpensive, selective, and sensitive nucleic acid. The utilization of dual quencher provided improved sensitivity, reduced background signal, and enhanced quenching efficiency [39].

In the present work, efforts have been made toward implementing gold nanoparticles and reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite (AuNPs@rGO) as dual quencher in the fabrication of fluorescence turn-on immunosensor for quantitative and highly sensitive CEA detection. The findings revealed that the AuNPs@rGO as a dual-quencher provided improved quenching and energy transfer efficiency (∼62 %, 88 %) compared to AuNPs (∼43 %, 81 %) as a single quencher. Further, AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite and protein functionalized GQDs (anti-CEA/amine-GQDs) based immunosensor has been utilized for CEA detection in standard and spiked serum samples.
The preparation of GO has been accomplished following an earlier reported article with minor modifications [42] (Supporting information, S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (amine-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Amine-GQDs were obtained using the bottom-up hydrothermal method following our previously reported method without further modifications [32] (Supporting information, S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-pot in-situ synthesis of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite was carried out following the one-pot in-situ low-temperature reduction method reported by Song et al. [43] with minor changes in reaction conditions and precursor concentrations. The optimal process includes the addition of 50 mL of well-dispersed 0.1 mg mL−1 GO solution with 2 mL of 5 mg mL−1 HAuCl4.3H2O solution under the magnetic string. To promote the interaction and attachment of gold ions on the GO sheets, the resultant solution was allowed to stir for 15 min and then kept undisturbed for the next 30 min. Afterward, 5 mL of 44 mM trisodium citrate was added dropwise to the previously prepared solution, and the resultant mixture was refluxed for 1 h. Upon cooling to room temperature, the obtained reddish-brown dispersion is centrifuged and washed with double distilled water to remove the impurities. Finally, the washed solution was dried at 80 °C to get the powdered AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and mechanism of fluorescent immunosensing platform for CEA detection
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate the anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO immunoprobe, amine-GQDs were biofunctionalized employing well-known EDC-NHS chemistry as per our previously reported method [32,44]. The carboxyl groups in monoclonal anti-CEA antibody were animated by preparing a uniform solution with 0.05 M NHS (activator), 50 μg mL−1 of anti-CEA, and 0.2 M EDC (coupling agent) in 1:2:1 ratio. The obtained anti-CEA solution was added to the same volume of amine-GQDs solution (100 μg mL−1). The resultant mixture was incubated under humid conditions for the next 2 h. The unbound antibodies were removed by ultracentrifugation and washing with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. Further, for CEA detection, 500 μL of the energy donor species (anti-CEA/amine-GQDs) were added to the optimal amount (200 μL, 60 μg mL−1) of the energy acceptors (AuNPs@rGO) species. After that, a fixed concentration of CEA antigen was introduced, and the corresponding changes in fluorescence intensity were computed by recording the fluorescence emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The immunoprobe was incubated for about 24 min before measurement to have the specific antigen-antibody interactions. The same approach was repeated for further studies with different CEA concentrations ranging from 0.001 to 1200 ng mL−1. The sensing mechanism of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO based fluorescent immunosensor is illustrated in Fig. 1 , consisting of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite as acceptor species and protein functionalized amine-GQDs as donor species. The anti-CEA/amine-GQDs donor species exhibit strong blue fluorescence around 450 nm upon excitation with UV radiation (360 nm). When AuNPs@rGO acceptor species are added to the donor species, the non-specific binding interactions and π-π stacking between anti-CEA/amine-GQDs and AuNPs@rGO bring the donor-acceptor pairs into NSET proximity to facilitate the substantial fluorescence quenching and energy transfer process. The AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite serves as a dual-quencher for highly efficient fluorescence quenching of donor species using the approach of multiple-donor-single-acceptor. The energy transfer to rGO and AuNPs has been indicated with black and red-colored arrows (Fig. 1). During the detection process, the addition of CEA antigen facilitated the binding of CEA with anti-CEA owing to the specific antigen-antibody interactions, which successively increased the distance among donor-acceptor pairs, thereby impeding the NSET process and resulting in the restoration of fluorescence intensity. The amount of restored fluorescence depends on the CEA concentration in the sample, thus providing the quantitative determination of the CEA biomarker. Besides, the specific antigen-antibody interactions ensure the specificity of the fabricated fluorescent immunosensor.

The real sample analysis of the fabricated CEA immunosensor was carried out with spiked serum samples. After clearance from the institutional Ethical and Biosafety committee (BT/IHEC-IITR/2019/7525) [45], blood samples were collected from healthy volunteers. The obtained samples were immediately transferred to the sterilized tubes and centrifuged at 4000 rpm to separate the serum from other biological content. After centrifugation, the obtained serum samples were diluted with PBS (1:5) and spiked with the known concentration of CEA (2, 5, 10, 20, and 50 ng mL−1). The repeated thawing process was avoided to maintain the structure of the CEA protein present in serum samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, optical, and morphological characterizations of GO and AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The successful synthesis of GO and the AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite was validated via different characterization techniques. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to determine the crystallinity of synthesized GO and AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite in the range of 5–80° (Fig. 2a). In GO, a distinct high-intensity peak at 2θ = 11.3 attributed to the (002) plane is found that shifts to 24.3 in the XRD spectrum of AuNPs@rGO owing to the in-situ reduction of GO to rGO during nanocomposite synthesis [44,46]. The XRD spectrum of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite reveals four additional crystalline peaks at 2θ angles of 38.1°, 44.3°, 64.5°, and 77.4° (JCPDS card no. 040784) corresponding to (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes, respectively, revealing the presence of metallic gold [35,43]. Further, UV–Vis spectroscopy was carried out to investigate the optical properties of GO and the AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite dispersed in double-distilled water (Fig. 2b). The UV–Vis spectrum of GO indicated two absorption peaks around 229 nm and 300 nm corresponding to π–π* transition and n –π* transitions of CC, and CO bonds, respectively. However, in the AuNPs@rGO absorption spectrum, the peak of GO around 300 disappeared, and the peak around 229 nm was red-shifted to 253 nm, indicating the successful in-situ reduction of GO to rGO, ascribed to the decreased gap between the valance and the conduction band and restored sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [47]. In addition, the AuNPs@rGO absorption spectrum indicated a specific surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak around 523 nm ascribed to the formation of AuNPs on rGO nanosheets [43,48].

The surface morphology, shape, and size distribution pattern of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposites and bare rGO nanosheets were studied using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 3 ). Image J software was utilized to measure the size of AuNPs distributed onto the rGO nanosheets. The average size of the AuNPs has been found to be 14.87 ± 1.76 nm, calculated using the size distribution histogram (Fig. S1). In addition, a narrow size distribution ranging from 11 to 20 nm has been observed. The TEM image of the bare rGO sheets (Fig. 3a) shows the presence of only thin graphene sheets. On the other hand, the TEM images of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposites (Fig. 3b-c) indicated the presence of uniformly dispersed AuNPs on the rGO nanosheets. The obtained TEM results verify the successful one-pot in-situ low-temperature reduction and growth of AuNPs onto the rGO sheets. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3d) reveals the existence of hexagonal, triangular, and spherical-shaped AuNPs. The formation of different structures may emerge as a result of the anisotropic growth during the nucleation phase [49]. It has been predicted that the T-sites, i.e., corner sites directly above C-atoms present on the surface of rGO, might serve as the most energetically favored nucleation sites for growth and deposition of the AuNPs due to the low migration energy (0.03 eV) and adsorption energy (0.8 eV) [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for CEA detection
ce:section.ce:para:
A series of experiments were conducted to optimize various sensing parameters, including concentrations of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite, pH of PBS solution, and the incubation time required for sufficient antigen-antibody interactions. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (QE) of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite was estimated using Eq. (1) [32].
(1)
QE =
F 0
− F /
F 0
where F0 and F correspond to the photoluminescence (PL) intensities of protein-functionalized amine-GQDs in the absence and presence of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite. The anti-CEA/amine-GQDs donor species were added with increasing concentrations of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite in the range of 0–100 μg mL−1, followed by recording their corresponding PL emission spectra. The PL intensity emitted by anti-CEA/amine-GQDs was found to decrease steadily under different concentrations of AuNPs@rGO (Fig. 4a). Alternatively, the QE increased with increasing concentrations of AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite and reached a plateau (∼88 %) (Fig. 4b) as the AuNPs@rGO concentration exceeded 60 μg mL−1, signifying the completion of the reaction between donor-acceptor pair. As a result, 60 μg mL−1 was chosen as the optimal concentration for the subsequent studies to avoid an excess of quencher in the sensing system. Compared to our earlier reported AuNPs as a single quencher (∼ 81 %) [32], the AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite exhibited improved QE owing to the higher surface-to-volume ratio and synergistic effect of AuNPs and rGO nanosheets. The non-specific binding interactions and π-π stacking between anti-CEA/amine-GQDs and AuNPs@rGO bring these donor-acceptor pairs into NSET proximity to induce substantial fluorescence quenching [37]. Following that, the characteristic Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. S3) and the time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. S4) were investigated to ascertain the quenching mechanism, viz. dynamic or static. In static quenching, a non-emissive ground state complex is formed upon mixing the donor species with the quencher species, and the corresponding fluorescence lifetime does not change with the quencher concentration. On the other hand, dynamic quenching occurs due to the collision between donor and quencher species, and the corresponding fluorescence lifetime varies with the quencher concentration. The presence of an almost linear plot and the decrease in the fluorescence lifetime upon adding the AuNPs@rGO quencher implies that the quenching process is predominantly dynamically controlled. Next, the quenching rate coefficient was estimated following the Stern-Volmer model [50]. The calculated value of the quenching rate coefficient was found to be 6.98 × 106 mL μg−1 s−1. The AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite serves as a dual quencher for the efficient fluorescence quenching of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs using the approach of multiple-donor-single-acceptor. The rGO nanosheets owing to enhanced π–π stacking capability, increased sp2/sp3 ratio, more structural defects, and reduced negative charge intensity, can serve as super nano-quencher, thereby providing improved QE [51,66]. In addition, the AuNPs can also cause the fluorescence to be quenched through radiative rate suppression. Further, the energy transfer efficiency (
ϕ E
) between AuNPs@rGO and anti-CEA/amine-GQDs was computed using Eq. (2)
(2)
ϕ E
= 1 −
τ DA
τ D
where
τ D
and
τ DA
represent fluorescence lifetime of donor species (anti-CEA/amine-GQDs) without and with quencher species (AuNPs@rGO), respectively. The calculated
ϕ E
was found to be ∼62 %. Compared to AuNPs as a single energy acceptor (∼43 %), the AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite exhibits improved energy transfer efficiency. The broad absorption spectrum of rGO (varying from 200 to 800 nm) permits the NSET to happen without the strict restriction of sufficient spectra overlap between emission and absorption spectra. Besides, compared to conventional ‘one-to-one’ FRET pairs, the rGO nanosheets provide the capability of loading multiple fluorescent donors simultaneously [28]. Apart from that, it has been mentioned that AuNPs do not possess a defined dipole moment, allowing for the energy transfer to AuNPs from any orientation of fluorophores following dipole-surface interactions [28]. Besides, the absorption spectrum of AuNPs features a broad cross-section near the plasmon resonance frequency, improving their energy acceptance capability [52].

Following that, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity was investigated in the range of 1–11 by recording the fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 4c). The PL intensity was observed to vary slightly within the pH range of 6–9. However, PL intensity decreases in both highly acidic and basic pH caused by the presence of H+ and OH− ions, which diminish the activity of biomolecules and obstruct the emissive states of amine-GQDs. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed around the neutral pH (7.4). Thus, throughout further analytical experiments, a PBS solution having pH 7.4 is used. Next, the optimal fluorescence recovery period (incubation time) is optimized by measuring the immunoreaction time, the time required for the ample interaction between the CEA antigen and anti-CEA antibodies for the anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO based fluorescent immunoprobe. To accomplish this, the immunoprobe was incubated with a fixed antigen concentration (50 μL, 1 ng mL−1), and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded over a regular span of 3 min. The results in Fig. 4d indicate that the fluorescence intensity recovered progressively, with a maximum value of around 24 min, after which the intensity was almost constant. As a result, the ideal duration for fluorescence recovery was set to 24 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO-based immunosensing platform
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimized parameters, PL spectroscopy was used to investigate the analytical performance of the anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO immunosensing probe for quantitative detection of CEA. As shown in Fig. 5a, the quenched PL intensity of amine-GQDs was restored gradually with increasing concentrations (0.001–1200 ng mL−1) of CEA antigen. This recovery in PL intensity was directly related to the addition of CEA concentration and might be interpreted by the formation of a specific CEA-anti-CEA immunocomplex. The formed immunocomplex expelled the anti-CEA/amine-GQDs donor species from the AuNPs@rGO acceptor species, which effectively increased the distance among the donor-acceptor pairs, preventing the NSET process and eventually resulted in the recovery of GQDs' quenched fluorescence. Further, the calibration plot between the log concentration of CEA antigen and recovered PL intensity was investigated (Fig. 5b). Good linearity was observed in the CEA concentration of 0.001–500 ng mL−1 and explained by linear Eq. (3).
(3)
I = 681.39
log 10
CEA
pg
mL
− 1
+ 711.06 , Regression coefficient
R 2
= 0.98

Following that, the lower limit of detection (LOD) was determined using Eq. (4) with a triple signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3).
(4)
LOD =
3 σ
S
where S denotes sensitivity (as determined by the slope of the calibration curve) and σ denotes the standard deviation. The LOD for anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO immunosensing probe was found to be 0.35 pg mL−1. The analytical performance of AuNPs@rGO nanohybrid-based fluorescent immunosensor was compared with earlier reported immunosensors, aptasensors, and biosensors for CEA detection (Table 1 ). The superior biosensing parameters, viz. enhanced sensitivity, lower LOD, and wider dynamic range, might be attributed to the synergistic effect of AuNPs and rGO nanosheets. The high surface area of AuNPs@rGO nanohybrid can provide increased biomolecule loading, resulting in more antigen-antibody interactions.

The fluorescence response of the amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO sensing probe toward CEA detection was carried out as a control study in the obtained linear detection range (0.001–500 ng mL−1) under similar conditions. The findings indicated that no significant change in PL intensity occurs with increasing concentration of CEA, implying that the recovery in the fluorescence intensity was entirely due to immunoreaction between anti-CEA antibodies and CEA antigens. Further, the selectively of fabricated fluorescent biosensing platform was investigated by determining the corresponding response (recovery in PL intensity) with different serum interferents, including cancer biomarkers (1 ng mL−1) viz. CEA, neuron-specific enolase (NSE), endothelin-1 (ET-1), cardiac troponin-I (cTnI), cytokeratin-19 fragment (CYFRA-21-1), protein sample (bovine serum albumin (BSA)), organic compounds (0.1 M)(ascorbic acid, folic acid, lactic acid, glucose), ionic solutions (0.1 M) (Fe+, Ca+, K+, Na+), and the mixture of all with CEA (1 ng mL−1). It was observed that compared to the blank sample, the magnitude of PL intensity scarcely changes with the addition of potential interferents. However, the PL intensity recovered significantly with the addition of the target CEA antigen attributed to the specific antigen-antibody immunoreactions. Besides, the selectivity coefficient (SC) was computed for every interferent utilizing Eq. (5). The SC value for each interferent was determined to be ∼1
(5)
SC =
I
C + I
I C
where IC and IC+I represent the amount of PL intensity in the absence and presence of each interferant, respectively. Following that, the fluorescence response of the immunosensor was recorded in the mixture of all interferents, including CEA. A substantial change in fluorescence recovery was obtained that was comparable to the analytical signal of the CEA biomarker. The obtained results indicated that the fabricated fluorescent immunosensing platform has strong selectivity for CEA detection. Subsequently, the reproducibility (Fig. S5) of this platform was determined by recording the fluorescence response of three different fluorescent probes, namely probe-1 to 3 fabricated by implementing a similar method and added with 1 ng mL−1 CEA antigen. An almost identical response was obtained with a percentage relative standard deviation (% RSD) of ∼4 %, signifying the notable reproducibility of the prepared immunosensing platform.

The real sample analysis of this immunosensing platform was carried out in CEA-spiked serum samples. The obtained serum samples from the institute hospital were diluted with PBS (1:5) and spiked with a known concentration of CEA (2, 5, 10, 20, and 50 ng mL−1). Under optimal conditions, 50 μL of spiked samples was added to the amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO sensing probe, and the corresponding changes in PL intensity were computed by recording their fluorescence emission spectra. Interestingly, a similar trend in fluorescence recovery with different CEA concentrations was observed for both standard, and CEA spiked serum samples. Besides, the calibration plot revealed the linear association of recovered PL intensity with the log CEA concentration (Fig. 6 ) as described by linear Eq. (6).
(6)
I = 624.37
log 10
CEA
pg
mL
− 1
+ 998.89 , Regression coefficient
R 2
= 0.98

The % recovery in PL intensity with CEA spiked serum samples was calculated using Eq. (S1) and listed in Table S1. The anti-CEA/amine-GQDs/AuNPs@rGO-based CEA immunosensor revealed % recovery varying from ∼92–98 % with an average recovery of ∼95 %, signifying the aptness of the sensing platform for quantitative CEA detection in clinical samples.

A label-free, selective, sensitive, rapid, and facile fluorescent immunosensor comprising protein functionalized graphene quantum dots (anti-CEA/amine-GQDs) and a nanocomposite of gold nanoparticles and reduced graphene oxide (AuNPs@rGO) as donor-acceptor pair has been demonstrated for CEA detection. The AuNPs@rGO nanocomposite was utilized as a dual-quencher that revealed improved energy transfer and quenching efficiency (∼62 %, 88 %) compared to single AuNPs (∼43 %, 81 %) quencher owing to the higher surface-to-volume ratio and synergistic effect of AuNPs and rGO nanosheets using the approach of multiple-donor-single-acceptor. Further, the developed immunosensor can detect CEA biomarker in the linear range of 0.001–500 ng mL−1, which covers the whole physiological secretion range of CEA in healthy and cancer patients. Besides, a good sensitivity of 681 mL pg−1 with remarkable reproducibility, specificity, response time (24 min), and lower LOD (0.35 pg mL−1) has been obtained. Further, the potential of fabricated immunosensor has been examined in CEA-spiked serum samples and revealed an average recovery of ∼95 %. Additional research efforts should be made toward exploring the potential of these GQDs and AuNPs@rGO dual-quencher-based platforms in developing fluorescent biosensing platforms for the efficient detection of various non-communicable and communicable diseases.

Ashish Kalkal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Rangadhar Pradhan: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Gopinath Packirisamy: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: AK thanks the Ministry of Education, Government of India (GOI), for the fellowship. GP is grateful to the Department of Science and Technology (DST), GOI, Technology Innovation Hub (TIH) for Devices Materials and Technology Foundation, IIT Roorkee [A Section-8 Company: Divyasampark IHub for Devices Materials and Technology Foundation]. Sincere thanks to Centre for Nanotechnology and Institute Instrumentation Centre, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, for the various analytical facilities provided.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.125157.
